questions.text: Which of the following acid can be used as a catalyst in Friedel Craft's reaction:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aluminium trichloride (AlCl3) is often used as a catalyst in Friedel-Crafts reactions since it acts as a Lewis acid and coordinates with the halogens, generating an electrophile in the process.

Nitric acid is a reagent and catalyst used in the functionalization of aromatic substrates. It can act as an acid catalyst or a dessicant depending on its interaction with solid acids. Nitric acid is a strong acid that acts as a nitrating reagent in organic chemistry reactions.
Beryllium chloride is used as a raw material for the electrolysis of beryllium, and as a catalyst for Friedel-Crafts reactions.

Sodium chloride (NaCl) was used as catalyst for the production of bulk chemicals of dimethyl carbonate and ethylene glycol by transesterification of ethylene carbonate with methanol first time in high activity with a TON of 770.

Chlorine withdraws electrons through inductive effect and releases electrons through resonance. Hence, chlorine is ortho, para-directing in electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.

questions.text: In toluene synthesis by Friedel Craft, the reactants in addition to anhydrous AlCl3 are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In the Friedel-Crafts synthesis of toluene, the reactants in addition to anhydrous AlCl3 are benzene (C6H6) and methyl chloride (CH3Cl). The correct option is d.Explanation:The Friedel-Crafts synthesis of toluene involves a reaction named Friedel-Crafts alkylation, which was discovered by Charles Friedel and James Crafts in 1877. This particular reaction allows for the formation of alkyl benzenes from benzene and an alkyl halide in the presence of anhydrous aluminum chloride (AlCl3) as a catalyst.However, it's worth noting that this reaction can sometimes lead to polyalkylation due to the produced alkyl groups being electron-donating, making the products more reactive towards electrophilic attack than the starting materials.For the synthesis of toluene, the correct reactants in addition to anhydrous AlCl3 are benzene (C6H6) and methyl chloride (CH3Cl). So, the correct answer is d. C6H6+CH3Cl.

questions.text: Which of the following is ortho and para directing group:

options.explanation: OH and - NHCOCH 3 are ortho and para directing groups. These are also the electron donating groups and the attack of the incoming groups is at the ortho and para position.

The resonance structure for the evidence of the methoxy group is ortho-para directing is given below: It is clear from the resonance structures that the methoxy group increases the electron density over the ortho and para positions of the ring, thus activating them to electrophilic attack.

-CHO group is deactivating in nature. Further the decrease in electron density is more at the o and p-positions therefore electrophilic substitution occurs only at m-position. Thus we can say that -CHO group in benzaldehyde is m-directing and deactivating.

questions.text: Structure of benzene is resonance hybrid of all_structures:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In Kekule's structure, the carbon atoms in benzene are arranged in a hexagonal ring with each carbon atom is attached to two other carbon atoms.The ring contains alternate single and double bond.These double bonds are due to the π electrons present in benzene.These π electrons can delocalize and form two structures with different position of a double bond.These are the resonance contributor. Thus, they contribute equally to form a resonance hybrid.

Thus, they contribute equally to form a resonance hybrid.

questions.text: Cyclic structure of benzene was proposed by:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: German chemist August Kekulé visualized the ring structure of benzene in 1865. Most organic chemical compounds contain loops of six carbon atoms called benzene rings. The nineteenth-century German chemist August Kekulé claimed to have pictured the ring structure of benzene after dreaming of a snake eating its own tail.

questions.text: Benzene does not undergo:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In benzene, the π-electrons are delocalised and makes the structure more stable. Delocalization of π electron is called resonance. Thus, benzene does not give addition reactions because of resonance stabilisation

Benzene is a planar molecule having delocalized electrons above and below the plane of the ring. Hence, it is electron-rich. As a result, it is highly attractive to electron-deficient species i.e., electrophiles. Therefore, it undergoes electrophilic substitution reactions very easily.
Benzene does not undergo polymerisation reaction due to the aromaticity of the ring. The conjugated, cyclic double bond system provides about 36 kcal/mol of stabilization to the molecule.

Nucleophiles are electron-rich. Due to the presence of an electron cloud of delocalized electrons on the benzene ring nucleophilic attack is difficult. Hence, they are repelled by benzene. Hence, benzene undergoes nucleophilic substitutions with difficulty.

questions.explanation: Benzene does not undergo polymerisation reaction due to the aromaticity of the ring. The conjugated, cyclic double bond system provides about 36 kcal/mol of stabilization to the molecule.

questions.text: The benzene molecule contains:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Benzene is an organic chemical compound with the molecular formula C6H6. The benzene molecule is composed of six carbon atoms joined in a planar hexagonal ring with one hydrogen atom attached to each. Because it contains only carbon and hydrogen atoms, benzene is classed as a hydrocarbon.

questions.text: The conversion of n-hexane into benzene by heating in the presence of Pt is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Isomerization is defined as the transformation of a molecule into a different isomer, and it can adopt two distinct conformations: cis or trans.

Aromatization is a chemical reaction in which an aromatic system is formed from a single nonaromatic precursor. Typically aromatization is achieved by dehydrogenation of existing cyclic compounds, illustrated by the conversion of cyclohexane into benzene. Aromatization includes the formation of heterocyclic systems.

Secondary amines have long been prepared by the dealkylation of tertiary amines using cyanogen bromide, the von Braun reaction. One of the best methods of dealkylating tertiary amines involves treatment with α-chloroethyl chloroformate, usually followed by methanolysis.
During a chemical reaction no atoms are created or destroyed. The atoms are rearranged. This results in the formation of new substances with different properties to the starting substances.

questions.explanation: Aromatization is a chemical reaction in which an aromatic system is formed from a single nonaromatic precursor. Typically aromatization is achieved by dehydrogenation of existing cyclic compounds, illustrated by the conversion of cyclohexane into benzene. Aromatization includes the formation of heterocyclic systems.

questions.text: The reaction mechanism of alkanes with halogens is known as;
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Propagation is not a complete mechanism but one of the steps in the free radical substitution process, where radicals react with stable molecules to form new radicals.

This is the correct answer. Alkanes react with halogens through a free radical substitution mechanism, especially under the influence of UV light. This reaction involves the substitution of hydrogen atoms in alkanes with halogen atoms.

Addition reactions are typical of alkenes and alkynes, where molecules are added across a double or triple bond. Alkanes do not have such bonds, so they do not typically undergo addition reactions with halogens.
Elimination reactions involve the removal of atoms or groups from a molecule, forming a double bond. This is not typical for alkanes with halogens; instead, alkanes undergo substitution reactions.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Free radical substitution.' This mechanism involves the substitution of hydrogen atoms in alkanes with halogen atoms through a free radical process initiated by UV light. The process is characterized by three steps: initiation, propagation, and termination. Propagation is only one part of this larger mechanism, not the mechanism itself. Addition reactions are not applicable to alkanes with halogens as they have no double or triple bonds. Elimination reactions involve the formation of double bonds, which is not the case in the halogenation of alkanes.

questions.text: The order of reactivities of the following alkyl halides for an SN2 reaction is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option suggests that the reactivity decreases as the size of the halogen increases, which is not accurate. In general, larger halogens (such as iodine) have weaker carbon-halogen bonds and are more reactive in SN2 reactions. b) RF > RBr > RCl > RI
This option suggests that the reactivity decreases as the size of the halogen increases, which is also incorrect for the reasons mentioned above.

This option suggests that the reactivity decreases as the size of the halogen decreases, which is not consistent with experimental observations. As mentioned earlier, larger halogens (such as iodine) have weaker carbon-halogen bonds and are more reactive in SN2 reactions.

The correct option (d) reflects the correct trend of reactivity in SN2 reactions, where alkyl iodides (RI) are the most reactive followed by alkyl bromides (RBr), alkyl chlorides (RCl), and alkyl fluorides (RF) in decreasing order of reactivity.

questions.explanation: The reactivity of the alkyl halide in the SN2 reaction is decided by the ease with which the halide leaves the substrate. The larger the size of the halide ion, the more is the leaving ability. As per the leaving ability, the order is I−>Br−>Cl−>F-. Hence reactivity order is RI > RBr > RCl > RF due to the higher stability of I- ion (due to its higher size). Hence, I ion is easily displaced by a nucleophile.

questions.text: RMgX is an organometallic compound, generally known as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Grignard's Reagent is an organometallic compound formed by reacting an alkyl halide with magnesium in dry ether. It is represented as R-Mg-X, where R is an organic group and X is a halogen.
Baeyer's Reagent is a dilute alkaline solution of potassium permanganate (KMnO4) used for the hydroxylation of alkenes, not related to organometallic compounds.
Ethers are a class of organic compounds with the formula R-O-R', where R and R' can be the same or different. They do not contain magnesium or halogens.
Esters are organic compounds with the general formula R-COO-R'. They are formed by the reaction of an alcohol with an acid, not involving magnesium or halogens.
Aldehydes contain a carbonyl group (C=O) with a hydrogen atom attached, represented as -CHO. They do not involve magnesium or halogens in their structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Grignard's Reagent, which is an important organometallic compound used in various organic synthesis reactions. It is typically formed by the reaction of an alkyl halide with magnesium in the presence of dry ether, resulting in the formula R-Mg-X. This reaction is vital in forming carbon-carbon bonds in organic chemistry.Other options, such as Baeyer's Reagent, Ether, Ester, and Aldehyde, are unrelated to the formation or properties of Grignard Reagent. Baeyer's Reagent is used in hydroxylation reactions, ethers are simple organic compounds without magnesium, esters are formed from acids and alcohols, and aldehydes contain a carbonyl group, none of which involve the specific organometallic bonding found in Grignard Reagent.

questions.text: SN2 reaction at an asymmetric carbon of a compound always gives:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Since the attacking nucleophile is not same as that of leaving group, the product cannot be enantiomer of the substrate

options.text: A product with opposite optical rotation.
options.explanation: Since the attacking nucleophile is not same as that of leaving group, the product cannot be enantiomer of the substrate so the product will not necessarily have opposite optical rotation.

Since the attacking nucleophile is not same as that of leaving group, the product cannot be enantiomer of the substrate so the product will not necessarily have opposite optical rotation. Moreover only one product is obtained, so we cannot obtain diastereomers.

SN2 reaction proceeds with inversion of configuration. Since the attacking nucleophile is not same as that of leaving group, the product cannot be enantiomer of the substrate so the product will not necessarily have opposite optical rotation. Moreover only one product is obtained, so we cannot obtain diastereomers.

questions.explanation: SN2 reaction proceeds with inversion of configuration. Since the attacking nucleophile is not same as that of leaving group, the product cannot be enantiomer of the substrate so the product will not necessarily have opposite optical rotation. Moreover only one product is obtained, so we cannot obtain diastereomers.

questions.text: The only o, p−directing group which is deactivating in nature is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The amino group (-NH2) is an ortho, para-directing group, meaning it directs incoming groups to the ortho and para positions of the benzene ring. However, it is a strongly activating group, not deactivating. It activates the ring through resonance and electron donation, making it a strong activator of electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

The hydroxyl group (-OH) is also an ortho, para-directing group. Similar to the amino group, it activates the benzene ring through resonance and electron donation. It is not deactivating in nature and therefore does not match the criteria for the question.

Halogens are ortho and para directors, but deactivators. Overall, they remove electron density from the ring as they are more electronegative than carbon, making them less reactive. However, due to their resonance donation to the ring ( which is because each halogen has a lone pair of electrons), if it does react, it reacts primarily at the ortho and para positions. Through resonance, a halogen tends to stabilize the carbocation formed during electrophilic substitution, and the effect is more pronounced at ortho- and para-positions. However, the inductive effect is stronger than resonance here and causes net electron withdrawal and thus net deactivation. The resonance effect tends to oppose the inductive effect for the attack at ortho- and para-positions and hence makes the deactivation less for ortho- and para-attack.

Alkyl groups (-R) are weakly activating groups, not deactivating. They donate electron density to the benzene ring through inductive effects, making the ring slightly more electron-rich. Alkyl groups do not have a strong directing effect and generally do not influence the position of incoming groups during electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

questions.explanation: Halides are ortho-para- directing groups but unlike most ortho-para- directors halides tend to deactivate benzene. This unusual behaviour can be explained by two properties:1. Since the halogens are very electronegative they cause inductive withdrawal (withdrawal of electrons from the carbon atom of benzene).2. Since the halogens have non-bonding electrons they can donate electron density through pi bonding (resonance donation).Option C is correct.

questions.text: Which alkyl halide has the lowest reactivity for a particular alkyl group?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Alkyl halides are compounds in which a halogen atom (F, Cl, Br, or I) is bonded to a carbon atom. The reactivity of alkyl halides generally increases in the order R-F < R-Cl < R-Br < R-I. This trend is due to the decreasing bond strength between the carbon atom and the halogen atom as the size of the halogen increases.
This is incorrect. R-Cl is more reactive than R-F.
This is incorrect. R-Cl is more reactive than R-F.
This is incorrect. R-I is the most reactive alkyl halide among the given options.

questions.explanation: This is the correct answer. Alkyl halides are compounds in which a halogen atom (F, Cl, Br, or I) is bonded to a carbon atom. The reactivity of alkyl halides generally increases in the order R-F < R-Cl < R-Br < R-I. This trend is due to the decreasing bond strength between the carbon atom and the halogen atom as the size of the halogen increases.

questions.text: Secondary alkyl halide is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Structure in Option A:The carbon bonded to chlorine (Cl) is also bonded to:One methyl group (-CH₃)Two hydrogen atoms (H)Since this carbon is attached to only one other carbon, it is classified as a primary carbon.Why Option A is a Primary Alkyl Halide:A primary alkyl halide (1°) is when the carbon attached to the halogen (Cl) is bonded to only one other carbon atom.In Option A, the halogen is bonded to a primary carbon.Therefore, Option A is a primary alkyl halide, not a secondary alkyl halide.
Option B:The carbon bonded to Cl is attached to only one other carbon and two hydrogens.This makes it a primary alkyl halide

Step-by-step Analysis of Option C:The carbon bonded to the chlorine (Cl) is also bonded to:One hydrogen (H)Two methyl groups (CH₃)This means the carbon attached to Cl is connected to two other carbon atoms, making it a secondary carbon.Since a secondary alkyl halide is defined as a compound where the halogen (Cl) is attached to a secondary carbon (which is bonded to two other carbon atoms), option C is indeed a secondary alkyl halide.
Option D:CH₃Cl (Methyl chloride) has only one carbon, so it does not fit primary, secondary, or tertiary classification.This is a methyl halide, not an alkyl halide

questions.explanation: Secondary alkyl halide is a halide in which Cl is attached to carbon which is attached to two carbon atoms. In this case, it is C. In A, carbon attached to halide is attached to a single carbon atom so it is primary alkyl halide. Similarly, in C and D carbon attached to chloride is attached to no carbon atoms which is also a case of primary alkyl halide.

questions.text: R-X on reaction with alcohols form:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: ROH (Alcohol) This is the starting material, not the product.

The correct answer is: ROR (Ether)Explanation:When an alkyl halide (R-X) reacts with an alcohol (ROH), it can undergo a Williamson ether synthesis (if reacted with a strong base) or other ether formation mechanisms. The general reaction is:R−X+R′−O−→R−O−R′+X−This reaction typically occurs in the presence of a strong base (like NaH or NaOH) to deprotonate the alcohol, forming an alkoxide ion (RO⁻).The alkoxide ion then performs an SN2 nucleophilic substitution on the alkyl halide (R-X), forming an ether (R-O-R').
R-X-OH (Not a valid compound) There is no stable compound with this structure in this reaction
RH (Alkane) Alkane formation would require a different reaction, such as Wurtz reaction or hydrogenation.

questions.explanation: We can deprotonate the alcohol to form an alkoxide anion RO- which is a much stronger nucleophile and will attack the alkyl halide forming an ether (Williamson Ether synthesis). This usually occurs through the SN2 mechanism.

questions.text: Alkyl halides involving -C-X bond breakage and -C-Nu bond formation simultaneously would follow the mechanism:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: SN1 (Unimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution) In SN1, C-X bond breaks first, forming a carbocation intermediate before the nucleophile attacks. It is a two-step mechanism, unlike SN2.

In SN2 (Substitution Nucleophilic Bimolecular) reactions, the C-X bond breaks and the C-Nu bond forms simultaneously in a single-step mechanism. The reaction follows a backside attack, leading to inversion of configuration at the carbon center (if it is chiral). The rate of reaction depends on both the alkyl halide and the nucleophile (Rate = k[Alkyl Halide][Nucleophile])
E1 (Unimolecular Elimination) In E1, the C-X bond breaks first, forming a carbocation, followed by loss of a proton (H⁺) to form an alkene. No simultaneous bond-making and bond-breaking as in SN2.

E2 (Bimolecular Elimination) E2 is a one-step elimination where a base abstracts a proton (H⁺) while the leaving group (X⁻) departs, forming an alkene. This is an elimination reaction, not substitution.

questions.explanation: The SN2 reaction is a type of reaction mechanism that is common in organic chemistry. In this mechanism, one bond is broken and one bond is formed simultaneously, i.e. in one step. It is a bimolecular reaction because the attacking Nucleophile and the leaving group are both involved in the rate-determining step.

questions.text: How would an ammino group affect the benzene ring for subsequent reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is incorrect because the ammino group does not deactivate the benzene ring; it activates it instead. Also, meta directing is associated with electron-withdrawing groups.

Activate the ring: The ammino group is an electron-donating group due to its lone pair of electrons. This donation increases the electron density on the benzene ring, making it more reactive towards electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Thus, the presence of –NH₂ activates the benzene ring.Ortho-para directing: The –NH₂ group directs incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to itself. This is because the lone pair on the nitrogen can participate in resonance with the benzene ring, stabilizing the positive charge that forms during the reaction at these positions. As a result, the ortho and para positions are favored for substitution over the meta position.
This is also incorrect for the same reason as above; the ammino group activates, not deactivates.
While it is true that the ammino group activates the ring, it does not direct substituents to the meta position.

questions.explanation: Activate the ring: The ammino group is an electron-donating group due to its lone pair of electrons. This donation increases the electron density on the benzene ring, making it more reactive towards electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions. Thus, the presence of –NH₂ activates the benzene ring.Ortho-para directing: The –NH₂ group directs incoming electrophiles to the ortho and para positions relative to itself. This is because the lone pair on the nitrogen can participate in resonance with the benzene ring, stabilizing the positive charge that forms during the reaction at these positions. As a result, the ortho and para positions are favored for substitution over the meta position.

questions.text: SN2 can be best carried out with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) is a type of nucleophilic substitution reaction in which the nucleophile attacks the carbon atom simultaneously with the leaving group, resulting in the substitution of the leaving group with the nucleophile. SN2 reactions work best with primary alkyl halides, as they have the least steric hindrance and are thus most accessible to the nucleophile. Secondary alkyl halides can also undergo SN2 reactions, but the reaction rate is slower due to the increased steric hindrance around the carbon atom. Tertiary alkyl halides are not suitable for SN2 reactions because of the high degree of steric hindrance around the carbon atom, which makes the reaction less feasible. Therefore, the best option for carrying out SN2 reactions is primary alkyl halides.

questions.explanation: SN2 reaction is most favoured by primary Alkyl Halides.This is because the primary alkyl halide provides minimum hindrance for SN2 substitution reaction.The order of reactivity of alkyl halide towards SN2 can be given as follow: 1∘ >2∘ >3∘

questions.text: Order of reactivity of halogens towards alkanes is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Alkanes undergo free radical substitution reactions with halogens, as they are sp3 hybridised and halogens can only be added in the molecule by replacing another group (substituting). The mechanism of free radical substitution involves 3 steps: initiation, propagation and termination. In the first step, the diatomic halogen molecules are split into two free radical species which can then add to the alkane molecules in the propagation steps. Down the group, the electronegativity decreases while the stability of these free radicals increases. The fluorine free radical is the least stable and most electronegative so it attacks the alkane more readily, and releases the highest amount of energy in the propagation step. The next is chlorine, followed by bromine and finally iodine, which is the least reactive. In Fact it is so unreactive that it is unable to extract a H atom from alkane. This order is consistent with option C, hence that is the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The order of reactivity of halogens towards alkanes, from highest to lowest, is as follows:1. Fluorine (F)2. Chlorine (Cl)3. Bromine (Br)4. Iodine (I)This order is based on the relative strengths of the carbon-halogen bonds in alkyl halides formed during the halogenation reaction. Fluorine is the most reactive halogen and is highly electronegative, leading to the formation of the strongest carbon-fluorine bond. Chlorine is less reactive than fluorine but still more reactive than bromine and iodine. Bromine is less reactive than both fluorine and chlorine but more reactive than iodine. Iodine is the least reactive halogen and tends to exhibit sluggish reactions with alkanes.

questions.text: Order of the reactivity of halogens during halogenation of alkanes is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not correct order.
This is not correct order.
This is not correct order.

The order of reactivity of halogens during halogenation of alkanes generally follows the trend: Fluorine (F) > Chlorine (Cl) > Bromine (Br) > Iodine (I)Fluorine is the most reactive halogen and tends to replace other halogens in a substitution reaction. However, due to its high reactivity, fluorination reactions are often difficult to control and can be hazardous.Chlorine is less reactive than fluorine but more reactive than bromine and iodine. Chlorination reactions are commonly used in industrial processes for the production of various organic compounds.Bromine is less reactive than both fluorine and chlorine. Bromination reactions are often carried out under milder conditions compared to chlorination or fluorination.Iodine is the least reactive halogen among the common halogens. Iodination reactions generally require high temperatures or catalysts to proceed at a reasonable rate.It's important to note that the reactivity of halogens can vary depending on specific reaction conditions and the nature of the alkane being halogenated.

questions.explanation: The chlorine radical is the most reactive of the among the given halogen molecules and it reacts easily with alkanes. In contrast, the iodine radical is the least reactive of the halogen radicals. In fact, it is so unreactive that it is unable to abstract a hydrogen atom from an alkane.So, (D) option is correct.

questions.text: Which type of alkyl halides gives SN2 mechanism?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: . The S N 2 mechanism is favored for primary alkyl halides because the carbon atom attached to the halogen is less sterically hindered c SN2 reactions, or Bimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution reactions involve two molecules, the nucleophile and the alkyl halide in the slow step of the process. The mechanism involves an attack from the nucleophile and simultaneous removal of the leaving group from the alkyl halide molecule. This happens for primary alkyl halides only because they have the least steric hindrance (as compared to tertiary or secondary alkyl halides) to allow the attack from nucleophiles. Therefore, option D is correct.

questions.explanation: The SN2 (Substitution Nucleophilic Bimolecular) mechanism typically occurs with primary (1°) alkyl halides. The SN2 mechanism involves a nucleophilic attack by a nucleophile on the electrophilic carbon of the alkyl halide, resulting in the substitution of the halogen atom with the nucleophile. In the SN2 reaction, the nucleophile approaches the carbon atom from the opposite side of the leaving group, leading to inversion of stereochemistry.The SN2 mechanism is favored for primary alkyl halides because the carbon atom attached to the halogen is less sterically hindered compared to secondary or tertiary carbon atoms. The steric hindrance created by bulky groups attached to the carbon atom can hinder the approach of the nucleophile and decrease the likelihood of an SN2 reaction.For example, an SN2 reaction can occur with a primary alkyl halide such as methyl chloride (CH3Cl):CH3Cl + Nu^- -> CH3Nu + Cl^-In this reaction, Nu^- represents the nucleophile, which can be an anionic species such as hydroxide ion (OH^-) or cyanide ion (CN^-).It's important to note that there are other mechanisms, such as SN1 (Substitution Nucleophilic Unimolecular) and E1 (Elimination Unimolecular), which occur with different types of alkyl halides depending on their structure and reaction conditions.SN2 reactions, or Bimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution reactions involve two molecules, the nucleophile and the alkyl halide in the slow step of the process. The mechanism involves an attack from the nucleophile and simultaneous removal of the leaving group from the alkyl halide molecule. This happens for primary alkyl halides only because they have the least steric hindrance (as compared to tertiary or secondary alkyl halides) to allow the attack from nucleophiles. Therefore, option D is correct.

questions.text: If any amino acids contain greater number of NH2, it is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Amino acids with a greater number of carboxyl groups (COOH) are considered acidic because the carboxyl group can donate a proton (H+) and become negatively charged.
Neutral amino acids have equal numbers of amino groups (NH2) and carboxyl groups (COOH). The positive and negative charges balance each other, resulting in a neutral overall charge.

Amphoteric amino acids can act as both acids and bases. They have both amino groups (NH2) that can act as bases and carboxyl groups (COOH) that can act as acids. Their behavior depends on the pH of the environment.

Amino acids with a greater number of NH2 groups (amine groups) are more basic because the amine groups can accept protons (H+ ions) and act as a base. The presence of more NH2 groups increases the basicity of the amino acid, as it can accept more protons.

questions.explanation: Amino acids with a greater number of NH2 groups (amine groups) are more basic because the amine groups can accept protons (H+ ions) and act as a base. The presence of more NH2 groups increases the basicity of the amino acid, as it can accept more protons.

questions.text: Classify this alkyl halide:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: An alkyl halide is classified based on the number of carbon atoms attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen. In this case, there is only one carbon atom attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen (-CH2-Cl), making it a 1°alkyl halide. The presence of a benzene ring does not affect the classification of the alkyl halide.

A 2° alkyl halide has two alkyl groups attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen. However, in the given diagram, there is only one carbon atom attached to the carbon atom with halogen, so it is not a 2° alkyl halide.

A 3°alkyl halide has three alkyl groups attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen. Similarly, in the given diagram, there is only one carbon atom attached to the carbon atom with halogen, so it is not a 3° alkyl halide.

A 4° alkyl halide has four alkyl groups attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen. However, in the given diagram, there is only one carbon atom attached to the carbon atom with halogen, so it is not a 4° alkyl halide.

questions.explanation: An alkyl halide is classified based on the number of carbon atoms attached to the carbon atom bearing the halogen. The presence of a benzene ring does not affect the classification of the alkyl halide.

Halogenation is the term which can be defined as a chemical reaction that involves the addition of either one or more halogens either to material or compound.The halogenation's stoichiometry and pathway depend on the functional groups and structural features of the organic substrate and the specific halogen. Also, inorganic compounds like metals undergo halogenation.What is Halogenation of AlIkanes?Halogenation of an alkane produces a hydrocarbon derivative, where either one or more halogen atoms have been substituted for the hydrogen atoms. Usually, alkanes are unreactive compounds only because they are non- polar and lack the functional group where the reactions can occur. Therefore, free-radical halogenation provides method by which alkanes are functionalized.Alkane halogenation is given as an example of a substitution reaction, which is a type of reaction that often takes place in organic chemistry. A substitution reaction is a chemical reaction, where a part of a small reacting molecule replaces either an atom or the atom's group on a hydrocarbon or its derivative.
Halogenation of ketone dont give alkyl halides.

questions.text: Markownikoff’s rule is best applicable to the reaction between
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Markovnikov's rule states that when an asymmetrical reagent is added to an asymmetrical alkene, then the negative half of the reagent will attach to the carbon atom containing fewer hydrogen atoms. Markownikov's rule apply only and only to ASYMMETRICAL reagent adding to ASYMMETRICAL alkene An asymmetrical alkene is one like propene or but-1-ene in which the groups or atoms attached to either end of the carbon-carbon double bond are different. For example, in propene there are a hydrogen and a methyl group at one end, but two hydrogen atoms at the other end of the double bond. An asymmetrical reagent is like having different atoms as HCl.
C2H4 is symmetrical because there are the same number of atoms around the double bond.So markownikov's rule cannot be applied.
Here Br2 is again symmetrical
Right option because HCl is unsymmetrical and C3H6 is also unsymmetrical.

questions.explanation: Markovnikov’s rule states that for a hydrogen halide being added to an asymmetric alkene, the hydrogen attaches itself to the carbon having more hydrogens whereas the halide binds to the carbon with the least hydrogens out of the two carbons formerly having a double bond. Option D is best suitable to display Markovnikov’s rule as we have an asymmetric alkene and hydrogen bromide undergoing electrophilic addition.

questions.text: The reaction of isobutane with an unknown halogen is catalyzed by light. Identify the halogen if the two major products seen in a white mixture, obtained are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When isobutane combines with Cl2 in the presence of UV rays, the mixture becomes white in color forming chlorobutane.
Bromine does not react significantly and when reacts forms colorless bromobutane.
Iodine does not react significantly with alkanes but when it does forms a pink iodobutane mixture.
Fluorine reacts significantly even in the absence of UV rays and forms yellow fluorobutane.

questions.explanation: What we are seeing is an example of free radical substitution in which Cl2, in the presence of UV light, breaks into free radicals which substitute one of the hydrogens of isobutane.Flourobutane= yellowChlorobutane= whiteBromobutane= colorlessIodobutane= pink

questions.text: SN2 reactions can be best carried out with:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Primary alkyl halides are least sterically hindered, making them ideal for SN2 reactions, which require a direct backside attack by the nucleophile.
While secondary alkyl halides can undergo SN2 reactions, they do so more slowly than primary alkyl halides due to increased steric hindrance.
Tertiary alkyl halides are highly hindered and typically undergo SN1 reactions instead of SN2 reactions.

options.text: Both primary and tertiary alkyl halides
options.explanation: Only primary alkyl halides favor SN2 reactions due to minimal steric hindrance, while tertiary favor SN1.

questions.explanation: SN2 reactions involve a nucleophilic attack on the electrophilic carbon, leading to the inversion of configuration. The reaction is sensitive to steric hindrance around the electrophilic center. Therefore, primary alkyl halides are most suitable for SN2 reactions as they present the least steric congestion, allowing for efficient backside attack by the nucleophile. In contrast, secondary alkyl halides present moderate steric hindrance, making SN2 reactions possible but less favorable compared to primary halides. Tertiary alkyl halides are too sterically hindered for SN2 reactions and instead undergo SN1 reactions. Thus, primary alkyl halides are the best choice for SN2 reactions.

questions.text: Which one of the following is NOT a nucleophile?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: (a) H₂S - NucleophileSulfur in H₂S has lone pairs of electrons and is less electronegative than oxygen, making it a good nucleophile.It can effectively donate its lone pair in nucleophilic reactions.

(b) BF₃ - Not a Nucleophile (Correct Answer)Boron in BF₃ has only six valence electrons, leading to its electron-deficient state.It acts as an electrophile, accepting rather than donating electron pairs.
(c) NH₃ - NucleophileNitrogen in NH₃ has a lone pair of electrons that it can donate in nucleophilic reactions.This ability to donate electrons makes ammonia a good nucleophile.

(d) CN⁻ - Strong NucleophileCN⁻ (cyanide ion) carries a negative charge and has a lone pair on carbon.The negative charge greatly enhances its ability to donate electrons, making it a very strong nucleophile.

questions.explanation: A nucleophile is an electron-rich species capable of donating electron pairs to electron-deficient species. In contrast, electrophiles are electron-deficient and seek electrons. BF₃ is not a nucleophile because it is electron-deficient and acts as an electrophile, accepting electron pairs into its vacant orbital. H₂S, NH₃, and CN⁻ all have lone pairs of electrons that enable them to act as nucleophiles by donating electrons in reactions.

questions.text: Which alkyl halide gives SN2 reactions?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This is not correct.Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.

Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.The general reaction for an SN2 reaction is:Nu- (nucleophile) + R-X (alkyl halide) → R-Nu (product) + X- (halide ion)Here, R is the alkyl group, X is the halide (e.g., Cl, Br, I), and Nu- is the nucleophile. For the SN2 reaction to occur, the alkyl halide should be primary (the carbon attached to the halide is only bonded to one other carbon) because steric hindrance is minimal in primary alkyl halides, allowing the nucleophile to approach the backside of the carbon more easily.Secondary and tertiary alkyl halides do not typically undergo SN2 reactions due to increased steric hindrance around the reaction center, making it difficult for the nucleophile to attack from the backside. Instead, secondary and tertiary alkyl halides tend to undergo SN1 (substitution nucleophilic unimolecular) reactions, which proceed through a two-step mechanism involving carbocation intermediates.

This is not correct.Primary alkyl halides typically undergo SN2 (substitution nucleophilic bimolecular) reactions. An SN2 reaction involves a one-step mechanism where a nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbon (carbon attached to the halide) from the backside, resulting in the displacement of the leaving group (halide ion) and inversion of the stereochemistry at the reaction center.
This is incorrect as only option B is correct.

questions.explanation: The SN2 reaction mechanism involves the nucleophilic substitution reaction of the leaving group (which generally consists of halide groups or other electron-withdrawing groups) with a nucleophile in a given organic compound. The SN2 reaction is most favored by 'Primary Alkyl Halides'.

Then the carbo-cation is attacked by the nucleophile. Finally, the deprotonation of the protonated nucleophile takes place to give the required product.

questions.text: Reduction of alkyl halides in the presence of Zn and mineral acid produces _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the reduction of alkyl halides (haloalkanes) in the presence of Zn and mineral acid involves the addition of hydrogen (H2) to the carbon-halogen (C-X) bond, resulting in the replacement of the halogen atom with a hydrogen atom. This process does not lead to the formation of alkenes, which are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond.
We can prove this by the reaction Alkyl halides (except fluorides) on reduction with zinc-dilute hydrochloric acid give alkanes.Zn +H2 → ZnH2ZnH2 + 2CH3Cl → ZnCl2 +2CH4

This option is incorrect because the reduction of alkyl halides with Zn and mineral acid does not produce alkynes. Alkynes are unsaturated hydrocarbons that contain at least one carbon-carbon triple bond. The reduction process only adds a single hydrogen atom to the carbon-halogen bond, forming an alkane.

This option is incorrect because the reduction of alkyl halides with Zn and mineral acid does not lead to the formation of alcohols. Alcohols are organic compounds that contain a hydroxyl (-OH) group bonded to a carbon atom. The reduction process in this case replaces the halogen atom with a hydrogen atom, forming an alkane, but does not introduce the hydroxyl group characteristic of alcohols.

questions.text: Which of the following catalyst is used in the Friedel craft reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Zinc chloride (ZnCl2) is not used as a catalyst in the Friedel-Crafts reaction. It is not typically involved in this specific reaction.
Potassium permanganate (KMnO4) is not a catalyst in the Friedel-Crafts reaction. It is an oxidizing agent and is commonly used in various oxidation reactions, but not in the Friedel-Crafts reaction.

Aluminum chloride (AlCl3) is the correct catalyst used in the Friedel-Crafts reaction. It is a Lewis acid catalyst and is commonly employed in this reaction to facilitate the addition of alkyl or acyl groups to aromatic compounds.
Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) is not used as a catalyst in the Friedel-Crafts reaction. It is mostly used as a catalyst in other chemical processes, but not specifically in the Friedel-Crafts reaction.

questions.explanation: Anhydrous Aluminum Chloride is the catalyst used in the Friedel-craft reaction.

questions.text: 1-Chloropropane and 2-Chloropropane are _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are positional isomers.Positional isomers have the same functional group (reactive group of molecules), different positions of functional group, and the same molecular (ex. alcohol, propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol, 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane).
1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are not chain isomers.
1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are not functional group isomers.
1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are not matamers.

questions.explanation: 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane are positional isomers.Positional isomers have the same functional group (reactive group of molecules), different positions of functional group, and the same molecular (ex. alcohol, propan-1-ol and propan-2-ol, 1-chloropropane and 2-chloropropane).
